And they represent those eight pillars of the inner and outer world. So watch, so let me just draw a line down the middle, I'm going to give you context there and then we'll dive right in. So we have the inner world of success on one side and you see me do this many times and you have the outer world on the other, right? But now I want you to see here how it actually in an arithmetic logical way, why it's so important, okay? So on the inner world of success we have one, two, three, we have four pillars or four distinctions that dictate this inner world of success and on the outer world of success we have one, two, three, four, five, one, two, three, four, one, two, three, four, am I fucking crazy? One, two, three, four, right? That was a great point, okay? Well on the outer world of success, okay? So there's four on each side, eight pillars. Now of course I could have divided these into sub pillars but I wanted to make it simple because sometimes when you make things too granular they just lose their power, they have no effect, right? Now we know from past trainings, right? It's very simple. You have state management, the ability to manage your physiological emotional state. Number two, you have your belief systems, that there are certain beliefs that drive you to success and certain beliefs that impede you from achieving success, right? Number three, you have your power of vision focus, the ability to create a fully integrated vision for the future and knowing how to focus on that in the proper way, bifurcated focus. I've spoken about it many times where you focus with laser-glide and position but one eye stands on the horizon for opportunity, all right? And then lastly we have your personal standards, right? What standard you hold yourself to? Do you demand massive success sales that you won't set up for less or like, yeah, yeah, I want to make a hundred grand a year. It's just not, your stand is not that high. Those are your four inner-world pillars and then on the outer world, it's just we have number one, we have entrepreneurship, right? The rules and strategies of business and entrepreneurship, right? Business has rules and if you don't know those rules, you're going to get your ass handed to you when you try to grow your business or if you're just in sales and you don't know those rules and you try to build a little operation within the company that you're working for, you'll get your ass slammed to you that way because remember, again, I'll say it one more time, as a salesperson, you are a business owner. You have to start thinking that way and if you don't, you're going to find yourself doing everything yourself, meaning all the non-sales related tests. This is what causes some people who are top-produced material, not to become top producers. They don't understand the business aspect of being a salesman. That's why I'm teaching that to you as well. Okay, it makes sense? It should, because it's pretty obvious. All right, so we have those rules and strategies of business and entrepreneurship, how to fail elegantly, how to succeed wildly. I'll get into them later. Next, we have the distinction of marketing, right? There are two types, offline marketing, online marketing. How do you bring in the right people? First, how do you identify the right people? Who's your best buyer? How do you target them and then bring them into your sales funnel in a cost-effective manner? Whatever that might be, meaning your funnel could be walking in the door of your business. It doesn't matter with phone, in person, it doesn't matter internet. All the same thing. That's a big caveat in a cost-effective manner. The old joke about that, I don't know if you guys probably haven't seen, I Love Lucy. It's this famous comedy back in the 60s and 50s, and they haven't seen the Lucy goes into business. She's always just fucking everything up and she's hysterical. Lucy will ball, right? She's explained to her husband how they're making these, I forgot what I was saying, these pies. In other words, marketing, because he was costing us so much to reach your customers, that she was losing money. That's marketing, offline, online. Then of course, the counterpart to that is sales, influence, and persuasion. How do you close the fucking deal? How do you take those people that the marketing team, your marketing plan, brought into your funnel, and how do you slam them close? That's the third distinction on the outer world. Lastly, one that I don't really spend much time on, because it's kind of like a different, it's not really what this training is about, but it's important for overall life wealth generation. I call it MSIs, and that means, what do you do with your money once you make it? Does your money sit idle in the bank earning 1% or 2%? Or do you know how to safely and smartly, but aggressively put your money to work to make you even more money? That's a powerful thing. Rich is having a lot of money. Wealthy is having time to enjoy the money. You're not constantly stressed out and working for that wealth. To achieve wealth, you'll notice that wealthy people typically have more than one source of income. What that second source or third source is, not the purposes of this conversation, but just understand whether it's in real estate, whether it's network marketing, whether it's online marketing, whether it's just some sort of side investment to venture capital, great business VC, being an angel investor, they have second and third sources of income. See, rich people, they play offense. Poor people worry about what they're going to lose. They play defense. Do you know that I never worry about? Of course, I always, listen, of course, I'm smart. I always worry about what's the worst that can happen. It's an analysis, but that's not my worry. My focus on how much I come on. I'm an offensive player. I look to mitigate my downside, of course. But what do you like? Do you play offense or do you play defense? Do you seize opportunity or do you tend to let opportunity pass you by? Are you a person of action? Do you take massive action? Or do you tend to sort of stay put, let inertia guide you? Do you believe that you're the master of your own destiny? Or does the world happen to you? So it's not a one or a 10. Where do you stand? Frankly, my beliefs are a 10. I have 10, my 10, 10, 10, I believe I'm capable of greatness. I'm worthy of it. I should be rich. You're going to find, I'm going to make you find your why, but for right now, I want you to be honest. And also, do you have you master this subtle art? It's a subtle lot of being able to, you know, focus where you want to go, not what you're afraid of, but also scan the horizon for opportunities that come along. My staff will tell you, because one thing I am good at is literally, I am like constantly seized right then. I am always integrating and seizing. I never look, we have, we have something, we're doing things one way, one day, and the next day, it's not something else. Not that we throw out what we're doing, we're adding things in, we're making it better, we're augmenting them, any possible opportunity. I'm constantly scouring the horizon for opportunity. And if it makes sense, not that I ever would throw the baby out. I'm not going to say, oh, that's our new program. No. But can I integrate that into what we're currently doing to make it even better, to make it a brighter, a more lucrative picture? Do you do that? Or do you find it difficult to do that? You end up focusing on your fears and where you don't want to go more than where you do want to go. Seriously. People, and that's, by the way, I understand that, that's human nature. I'm going to knock that shit out of you in 2019, because that's that you can't succeed at the highest level like that. But I want to know. I want to know and I want you to know. I want you to really get, this is your chance to get out of it. It's an honest, moral, and business inventory. What are you like? What, you know, it's very easy for us to say what we're great at. How about what we suck at, you know, come on, let's, this is your chance. No one's going to see it. So be honest, give yourself a score. If you are strong in this area for vision focus, give yourself a 10. If you're on the weaker side and you're really struggling with this stuff, give yourself a one or two or three. Right? Do that now. 10 seconds. Next, your thermostat, your standards, right? Your standards is on some level, almost the opposite or the counterpart to state management, right? State management is about how I feel in the moment. And based on how I feel, based on my emotional state, can I access the resources, the talents, the skills, the things I've mastered, is the spicket open and my talent and resources flow out and I can use them effectively or am I blocked? Is the spicket closed because I'm feeling fearful and certain and angry and impulsive and overwhelmed? Close spicket, clarity, courage, fearless, certain, open spicket, overwhelmed, fearful, greedy, close spicket. That's one aspect, right? The other aspect that's in the moment, but the long term is where must I end up to feel good? What's my standard, right? And I've been through this many times. It's standards in terms of like, you know, like the thermostat in your house. It's set at 72, right? If the house drops below 72, the boiler kicks on and heats up the house like crazy. But if it gets to 73, what happens? It's shut the boiler, the thermostat shuts off, the boiler closes down, the house starts to cool down. The same thing happens with a thermostat for your success. Every human being has a number that they feel comfortable at and that, you know, that what that number is, that's that level that if you're below it, you'll be fucking running like crazy to try to get back after that number. If you go above that number, you'll slow down. The problem that I see all over the world and I saw it especially get strattened in 1988 was when I discovered the straight line, it was a game changer as a skill set. Meaning it could take anyone and allow them to become rich, make a million bucks a year or more back then even, right? The problem with that was if those kids that came to me, they mostly get standards that were like, well, if I make 75 grand a year, I'm fucking on easy street. I had to raise that shit up because what would have happened is they would have all hit that in the first week of the year. Okay, ready? We're back. Hey guys, sorry about that because Bowen's not here. These guys didn't know that. The days, Dan is on his first life, he's on his phone, he's on Wi-Fi. We don't use Wi-Fi. We take a direct connection from the internet so it's a hard line so we don't have these problems anymore. Sorry about that. Anyway, so we were on thermostat and I was saying is that in other words, if you're thermostat to success, let's stratten these guys so they make 80 grand, 70 grand, they're happy, right? That wouldn't have worked for us. I had to raise their standards and I'm going to get into this exercise with you either before the end of the year or really early next year. We've got to raise your standards. Don't serve you. We've got to raise those standards. Now again, there's no right or wrong but your vision and your standards must be congruent. You can't have a champagne vision and have beer standards. Not going to work very well. You'll be, you know what that is? That's an ineffectual dreamer. You dream about this vision but you're not willing to do the work. Your stand doesn't let you do the work. Conversely, you might have a champagne standard but a beer vision. You know what you then, you're an anxious, you're an achiever. You have to make money. I don't know how and I'm working really hard but you don't feel good about it. You get it? So they should be, it's the right or wrong. What's your stand and what's your vision? They're personal to you but they better match. They better be congruent. Now on the world of entrepreneurship, okay, I want you to essentially start with number one. Do you know how to fail elegantly? Do you know how to essentially start a business, operate a business, test out new ideas in a way that when you're wrong, they don't work out, you minimize on the downside and then you maximize on the upside of the lessons learned. So you minimize on the loss of time and money but you learn from that because you always learn from those mistakes. That's how you fail elegantly. Things like starting with low overhead, focusing on cash flow versus price, okay? Not hiring all sorts of people for different tasks, right Adelaide because you just know you're going to succeed, okay? Renting versus buying or leasing versus buying. There's all these different rules I teach when it comes to entrepreneurship of how to fail elegantly. It doesn't matter what they are, this is just finding out where you are right now. Do you know how to be wrong or do you simply bet the kitchen sink on everything, right? When you have an idea and if you're wrong, you get fucked, all right? That's one side. And he's an incredibly brilliant guy. He taught me a process for changing limiting beliefs. Now, let me give you a practical example. And my first business went bust. It was meat and seafood. I was 22 years old. I lost everything. And then I lost everything. I went bankrupt, right? 26 trust trucks were repossessed. I had to go bankrupt, right? I went down to Wall Street. And then after a year there, I had an opportunity because I was a great salesman, so I noticed and offered me the ability to start my own brokerage. I said, listen, you're so great at selling and training, you know, I'll do all the back office stuff and I'll do the trading, you do the sales training and we'll make millions, right? And I said, nah, I'm not interested because I said to myself, maybe I'm not entrepreneurial because of that one failure in the meat business. Get it? See, so even I am no different than anyone else. I develop limiting beliefs too. But what separates me from most people is that I am able to go through, I end up going through my own, I step through, I don't let them hold me back. I go forward, I act in spite of them because my other drivers, my standards, my state, my skill sets, they're so high, I'm able to battle through my beliefs. And then I change, I self change them by doing that and creating new experiences that serve as anchors for why the old belief was wrong. That's a really slow, tough way to do it. In other words, it's easy to be in a positive state when everything's going great. It's a lot harder when the shit's hitting the fan around you.
